transcript:
Two swarms of light are pouring out from the center — one cyan, one amber — each tracing its own kind of motion. Watch closely in the first few seconds: the cyan particles move with a strange, looping certainty, while the amber ones twitch and respond, like they're feeling their way forward.

That's not just style — it's built into the physics. The cyan population runs on a function literally called "compute what already exists": every path is fully calculated before the animation even starts, down to the frame. The amber population uses "sample local conditions," recalculating its direction every instant from a flow field. One is memory, one is invention — until you hit the variable called dissolution, which slowly force-blends both colors toward a shared pale violet called "dissolved." By the thirty-second mark, that convergence is total — the code stops asking which particle is which.

What gets me is that the paths themselves never change — the retrieved ones are still frozen, the generated ones still reactive. Only the color forgets the difference. The distinction was real in the mechanism the whole time; it just stopped being visible.
